target.name: StoreDot
target.founder: Doron Myersdorf, Simon Litsyn, Gil Rosenman

methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series E → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.4x) to $206M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Climate & Energy (found 0 same-stage, 2 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 19 same-sector peers exist in Climate & Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

data.narrative:
StoreDot, a Series E frontier-tech company in Climate & Energy, has raised $206.5M to date across its funding rounds. The relevant peer universe comprises 9 private companies and 1 public comparable (Linamar, LNZA), though narrow-filter peer matching within the sector proved limited due to stage and valuation dispersion.

The Stage-Progression method is applicable here, using total capital raised ($206M) as a proxy baseline and applying a 1.4x step-up multiplier typical for Series E advancement. This yields an implied valuation of $289M. A Peer-Median approach was not executable—while 19 same-sector peers exist, filtering for identical stage, adjacent stage, or similar valuation tier across the broader Climate & Energy universe produced fewer than three datapoints, making median-based triangulation unreliable. Relying on a sector-wide median would obscure material differences in company maturity and capital intensity.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $246M (low) to $332M (high), centered on a $289M median. This range reflects ±15% variance around the stage-progression result. Key uncertainties include the applicability of historical Series E multipliers to frontier-energy dynamics and the absence of tight peer comparables in battery and energy storage at identical stages of maturity. Further refinement would require additional late-stage private or public comps in the adjacent battery-technology and advanced-materials space.
